sentences.text: So what we also liked to do was to-- to prepare the seder itself, to help my grandfather and my grandmother to prepare the seder itself.
Sometimes we broke things, but that has nothing to do.
And my grandfather lived across the synagogue, across the shul, so it was very easy for us straight from the shul to go in.
It was a lot of light, not electricity.
There was no electricity.
There was-- there weren't even gas lamps.
There was petroleum lightning that we had there.
And it was a very warm atmosphere.
And my-- there is-- until nowadays, there is a [NON-ENGLISH] Pesach that my grandfather used to sing.
And we sing it regularly at our seder in his memory.
You want it?
OK.
That's-- you know, it's not like actual music.
That's very old, slow kind of music.
[NON-ENGLISH SINGING] And so on.
There was no name of the shul.
There was no need for a name, because there was only one shul.
And this-- what I remember, the cheder was, by the way, in hte shul.
So we went there daily.
But what I remember, that one of the people living in that village he drew-- he make-- make a drawing of a big lion in the shul.
And we all went to see this fantastic thing, a lion.
I mean, to see a lion, we didn't have books and all these kind of things.
We just knew that there is a lion.
So we saw a lion.
There was no rabbi.

The leading religious figures in that village, in that shul, were my grandfather, who finished the Talmud Bavli a certain something times, learned up to the end of his years-- maybe we'll come to that-- and my father, who finished during his lifetime 20 and something times the Talmud Bavli.
So they were the leading figures.
They were not-- they weren't rabbis.
They didn't even want to be a rabbi.
When we moved to Vatra-Dornei, that was a totally different setting.
First of all, it was a little town.
There were streets.
There were paved streets.
There was a huge park, a beautiful park.
There were two rivers in that place.
The rivers were the Dorna and the Bistrita.
They were the two rivers.
They joined next to this city each other.
The Dorna went into the Bistrita.
There were mountains.
Winter time, there were ski, sliding-- ice sliding and-- how do you call-- sleighs on the snow.
And summertime, people came from all over the world, like to Switzerland, like to St.
Moritz.
So we knew there a lot of people of-- some of them prominent people.
During summer, we could have a few tens of rabbis or rabbis, big rabbis, famous people.
[NON-ENGLISH] used to come every year to our-- to our city.
[NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], I could mention a lot of them.
We had the chief rabbi of Romania, Rabbi Niemirower, who came to our place in certain years.
Came people from Israel like Rabbi Fischman, Rabbi Neufeld.
And [NON-ENGLISH] came to that place on the summer, because there were about 10,000 people who used to come to this place during the summer.
So I heard there the famous Cantor of Tschernowitz, Moshe [?
Steinberg, ?] Sholom Katz who was then cantor in Washington, and [NON-ENGLISH], who passed away in Israel, was cantor from [NON-ENGLISH] and so on and so on.
All these figures, we had them there handy and without paying entry.
So that was a total different thing.
Then we had a cheder.
There was in that place-- there was a school called [NON-ENGLISH], the Hebrew School, in which they studied at least partially in Hebrew.
But that was a school recognized by the states.
But I didn't go to the [NON-ENGLISH] shul.
It wasn't religious enough, so I went to the cheder.
And because of this, I never went even to public school.
That was my-- my-- my studies were all in the religious area.
We had a cheder in the courtyard of the temple of the place.
There was a beautiful temple.
Of the temple?
The temple.
There was no-- no name.
There was der temple, or the das temple.
I don't remember.
I think the der temple, but maybe I'm wrong already.
And there we had a [NON-ENGLISH], a teacher.
And I would like to mention his name, because he was a wonderful person and he perished also in Transnistria.
His name was Rabbi Elimelach Fogel, F-O-G-E-L.
And we studied there [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH], you name it.
And with my father, I started even [NON-ENGLISH], just because my father was an expert in this field.
And the book I studied from is called [NON-ENGLISH] or [NON-ENGLISH], which was a famous book in this area.
Now, I used to play a lot.
I played soccer.
I was the goalkeeper usually, because I was probably very agile, so I could jump to catch the balls, et cetera.
But I wasn't so good in shooting in the goals.
[LAUGHS] And that was a very-- when we had time, we used to go and play soccer.
There was a-- a stadium in our place.
And during the summer, there were football teams-- we call that football, you call it here soccer-- football teams who came to play there.
And we used even to-- to run away from the cheder in order to watch the match, but that's a different story.
[LAUGHS] That is-- so the life was totally different.
Another thing, we had there two youth movements, one which was [NON-ENGLISH], and the other one which was [NON-ENGLISH].
And we-- it started for me as the first, but I was a child at that time.
We started a religious youth movement, [NON-ENGLISH], who became a steady youth movement in that place.
With that, first of all, I was a member.
That was the [? weirdest ?] thing.
And then, this was organized in the late '30s by the man who became later on my brother-in-law, Reuven Feuerstein.
He was at that time a guy of 18-something years old.
He was already the older one.
And I was at that time 11 and 1/2 or 12 or something like this.
And he mobilized me, as well as together with other children, and we founded the [NON-ENGLISH] movement and we kept to this even during the concentration camps and later on.
There is, by the way, very interesting thing.
My grand-- my father [NON-ENGLISH] was a good friend with the general who was the commander of the city, because they used to go together to the shvitz [NON-ENGLISH].
I know you call that to the sauna.
The sauna.
Yeah.
That's--
But we called it shvitz [NON-ENGLISH].
And they liked each other very much.
And at a given moment, he got a message from this general that the police is looking after this guy, Feuerstein, who is doing subversive work here.
So my father called me and told me, could you please find him and put him on the train that he should leave the city-- the town immediately?
So I went and I found him somewhere in the park.
And he had some little lists of names with the children that he recruited for the movement.
So he had also an open shoe.
So he put this little piece of paper in his shoe, which was torn up a little bit.
And I brought him to the train.
I watched him until the train left the station.
And at that time, they didn't send the police from one place to another to look where he went.
But that was it, and I saved him at least from some serious troubles.
First of all as a happy young person.
I didn't like to study, but I caught very rapidly things, so I wasn't a bad student.
But to invest, that wasn't my-- my specialty.
I liked a lot to play games.
I was always social.
And I had friends, a lot of friends.
I liked to play ping pong at that time and had a good friend from a non-religious family that I played a lot of ping pong with him.
And we used to get together.
At that time, we used to have evenings of Yiddish literature.
There were readings, public readings of [NON-ENGLISH] or of [NON-ENGLISH] or of Sholom Aleichem, all kinds of things that are being recited.
That was a very interesting cultural activity we had.
I liked to watch my father and then later to join my father and my grandfather who were standing in the dining room having a book of notes, of musical notes of different prayers, singing those notes.
That's the way I still know [INAUDIBLE] until nowadays, despite the fact I-- I am not a musician.
We liked to have guests in the house.
And then we did all kind of things that my parents asked us to.
But mostly our life turned around our studies.
I think that I had no plans, simply.
I didn't think to it.
I was so involved in the actual life that to plan what will be when I'll be-- I assume that I was almost sure that I'm going to end up something similar to my father.
But I didn't plan.
You have to-- you are talking about someone who is just at the end of [INAUDIBLE] I was just touching adolescence, so these-- [DRINKS]
First of all, we had negative relationships.
For instance, I used to visit my grandparents' parents in Balaceana.
And one of my relatives was a strong guy.
He was considered to be the third strongest guy in the village.
I-- I don't know by what criteria.
But we liked to go with him, because all the [NON-ENGLISH] were afraid of him.
And when they started with us, he used to run after them.
They used to run away.

But I had all kinds of such incidents with [NON-ENGLISH] in the village who tried, some of them, to pull my payot-- I had payot at that time-- or just to call me names or just to chase me or just to run after me.
But at that time in our little town, I don't remember having any special relationship with non-Jewish, except for the guy who was the housekeeper, the keeper of the courtyard, who was non-Jewish.
And he probably afterwards-- after we were deported, he took away all our posessions.
But that's another story.
His name was [?
Stefan. ?]
My home, the basic language was Yiddish.
We all spoke also Hebrew.
My mother did not speak at that time Hebrew.
She learned that later.
But with my father, I could speak in Hebrew.
We spoke German, because that's-- Bukovina is a German area.
And I also-- we also spoke Romanian to some extent.
My first lecture in Hebrew language was from my uncle Zev, who was a [NON-ENGLISH] in that time in this little village of Arbore.
And he talked me-- he-- he taught me dikduk, or grammar, from a book called [NON-ENGLISH].
I remember still this book.
And I still remember some grammar rules from that time.
I don't remember where I put my glasses.
In-- in Arbore, I don't think I ever saw newspapers.
In Vatra-Dornei, there was a newspaper shop, a stationery as you call it here, led by a man called [?
Landau. ?] I forgot his first name.
And there, we used to get Romanian press, German press, like the [GERMAN], the [GERMAN], and Jewish or Yiddish press from Russia, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH].
And we read a lot of it.
We were well informed what's going on in Jewish life and in the world also.
We knew when Hitler came to power.
We knew what happened in Germany.
I think we knew about the Kristallnacht.
I'm not so sure.
But we were mostly centered about anti-Semitism in Romania itself.

I remember one thing in 1938-- I hope I'm not wrong-- or the end of '37, King Carol II, the King of Romania, appointed as prime minister Octavian Goga, and with him his-- his colleague, Cuza, they were the Nazi Party.
The Nazi party were called the Cuzaists.
And they started with a lot of anti-Jewish laws.
I do not remember any of the laws.
I think it had to do with certain restrictions.
You had to speak only Romanian and all kind of things.
They were probably Shabbat-- that you had to keep your-- your-- your shops open on Shabbat.
All kind of these things they started.
For one or another reason, the King decided that he had enough of them.
And he fired them.
And I remember it was the day of Purim, around 3:00 or 4 o'clock in the morning.
My late father wakes me up and tells me [NON-ENGLISH].
That was the sentence, that they went to hell.
And that was a big Purim.
Every-- we have always joy and fun on Purim, but that was especially joyous Purim.
So that was about the Nazis in Romania.
But there was also another very important Nazi figure, which was even worst.
And his name was Corneliu Codreanu, C-O-R-N-E-L-I-U, Corneliu, Codreanu, C-O-D-R-E-A-N-U.
He was called The Captain.
And his movement was called Gardi de Fier, G-A-R-D-I D-E F-I-E-R, which means the Iron Guard.
They wore green blouses.
And they were expressed Nazis Party, the most dangerous.
They even wanted to exterminate physically Jews.
And at a given moment, I think it was in 1940-- or I think it was 1940, they made a revolution.
It was January or February, 1940.
They made a revolution against the government.
At that time it wasn't anymore the King.
I think it was already a kind of a Republican government.
But I'm not sure about that.
In any case, during these few days that they-- they occupied the-- the-- radio station and some other public buildings in Bucharest.
They had a pogrom in Bucharest where a few hundred Jews were killed, and in many other places.
I jump a little bit over because of that question.

But in-- in our city during the [INAUDIBLE] in our town, they gathered all Jews between 18 and 50 in the courtyard of the synagogue and they put around [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] fire-- [NON-ENGLISH], guns, automatic guns.
And I won't forget the night that was the night where my grandfather was elder than 70 years.
He-- than 60-- than 50 years.
And he was not brought into that courtyard.
He called me and he asked me if I could sneak in-- into the courtyard of the synagogue to bring something to my-- to his children.
He had three children at that time.
Because I had studied in that cheder, I knew exactly how to get in and how to get out.
I succeeded to get in.
And he-- I did what I had to do and I went out.
We didn't know what would be the end of this.
They were really supposed to be exterminated somehow.

Fortunately enough, in our place lived a Jew in the name-- his name was Nathan Klipper, N-A-T-H-A-N, Klipper, K-L-I-P-P-E-R, who was a non-religious Jew that had little-- little relationship with the synagogues in the-- in our place.
But he was a wonderful Jewish person.
And he had some-- he dealt with German officers, because he-- he was in the wood-- wood industry.
And he had some business with German officers.
He came in the morning with a general, a German general , who freed all the Jews and gave the order to the Romanian army and to the-- to those [NON-ENGLISH] to leave the place.
And that was it for that time.
And I think it-- I am happy to mention the name of Nathan Klipper, who did a lot of good things to our community [? especially. ?] [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] OK.

By the way, these [NON-ENGLISH], they also killed-- they assassinated the Romanian prime minister, who was quite a democrat-- he was the name of Armand Calinescu, A-R-M-A-N-D, Calinescu, C-A-L-I-N-E-S-C-U-- about two days before Yom Kippur.
But at that time, the-- the Romania army reacted so strongly that they executed thousands of [NON-ENGLISH] in a few days.
And the general who did it, his name was [NON-ENGLISH] and we used to call him [NON-ENGLISH].
Except for these, not.
Perhaps one thing that-- but that was after I left Arbore.
The goyim wanted to-- to-- that the Jews should leave this little place, so they used to throw in into their houses-- we called it bombs, but they were-- they were hand grenades to explode.
The damage was more than physical casualties.
But this really caused that the Jews in many, many of those villages left to the cities.
My own family was not anymore there.
We went earlier to the Bessarabia, to Sculen.
But my grandfather, he came over-- he sent over first his son Shimon.
And he opened up a business in Vatra-Dornei.
And then he came over to Vatra-Dornei and lived in Vatra-Dornei.
Not as far as I can remember.
I mean, in Arbore.
But in Vatra-Dornei, there were some people who came from other places.
I would like to mention one name in this respect.
And that is the name-- a man by the name of Maurice Rath, M-A-U-R-I-C-E Rath R-A-T-H.
He somehow came to our city from Poland.
He didn't have any relatives wherever that I-- we know of.
We don't know who was his family.
But he rented in a little apartment in-- in the courtyard of our-- where we were living.
And so he was invited to our house.
He became a guest, and then he became a good friend, and then he became a part of our family.
And when we were deported to the concentration camps, he came with us, lived with us until the winter of 1942.
And he died on a typhus in Mogilev-Podol'skii in that concentration camp that we were.
He died almost on my hands.
He asked for a glass of water.
They brought him.
They wanted to give him to drink.
He just passed out.
Our area was not occupied by the Russians.
Therefore, we-- when the-- when the Germans invaded Russia, we still-- that was in June, 1941.
We still lived in our place until October.
Because those-- those parts which were occupied by the Russian before the invasion, the great majority of them were just exterminated.
